did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized deep learning models, specifically Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2, to detect and classify skin cancer (actinic keratosis and melanoma) from images. These AI models were trained on a dataset of 600 images and implemented in an Android application for mobile-based screening.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a mobile skin cancer detection application using Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 models. It details specific methods including data collection (600 images), model training parameters, and experimental results comparing accuracy on Jupyter notebooks versus Android smartphones, which classifies it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Development of Mobile Skin Cancer Detection using Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 Model
journal: Proc. of 2020 7th Int. Conf. on Information Tech., Computer, and Electrical Engineering (ICITACEE)
brief_summary: This study developed a mobile application for early skin cancer detection using Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 deep learning models to identify actinic keratosis and melanoma. The models were trained on a dataset of 600 images sourced from the ISIC archive, divided into two classes with 70% used for training and 30% for testing. Evaluation was conducted via Jupyter notebook testing on original images and smartphone camera testing on additional images. The Faster R-CNN model achieved the highest accuracy of 87.2% during Jupyter testing, while MobileNet v2 demonstrated comparable high accuracy of 86.3% when deployed on an Android smartphone. The results indicate that both models are effective for mobile-based skin cancer screening, with Faster R-CNN showing superior performance in offline testing.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two skin cancer types used in the study. The dataset contained 300 images of actinic keratosis. It is described as being triggered by ultraviolet (UV) radiation with color properties of pink, brownish, or a compound of these colors.

One of the two skin cancer types used in the study. The dataset contained 300 images of melanoma. It is described as the most malignant skin cancer, identified by UV light exposure and hereditary factors, with color properties consisting of more than one color.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates and class labels for skin cancer lesions (actinic keratosis and melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper utilizes Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 to detect skin cancer objects in images. Faster R-CNN is explicitly described as having a Region Proposal Network (RPN) that generates bounding boxes to recognize objects and fully connected layers for object classification. The study involves determining Region of Interest (ROI) coordinates for cancer targets and evaluating the models' ability to detect these specific lesion classes.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Actinic Keratosis vs. Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The models classify detected objects into two discrete classes: actinic keratosis and melanoma. The paper reports accuracy metrics for these class predictions, but the primary architectural function described (especially for Faster R-CNN) is object detection involving localization and classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 600 skin cancer images obtained from the ISIC archive website. The images were annotated with Region of Interest (ROI) bounding boxes using LabelImg to generate XML files containing coordinates. The dataset was divided into two classes: actinic keratosis and melanoma, with no regard for gender, age, or other factors.

use.training: Used for training the Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 models. The 420 training images were further split into 90% for training (378 images) and 10% for validation (42 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (42 images derived from the training split).
use.testing: Used for testing the models via Jupyter notebook on original images (120 images derived from the 30% test split). Additionally, 40 additional images not in the dataset were used for smartphone camera testing.
use.other: 40 additional images not in the main dataset were used for testing using an Android-based smartphone camera.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were compared using accuracy metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive Dataset (Jupyter Notebook T.description: Testing performed on original images loaded into Jupyter notebook using 30% of the dataset (40 images: 20 melanoma, 20 actinic keratosis).
ISIC Archive Dataset (Jupyter Notebook T.notes: Faster R-CNN produced higher accuracy than MobileNet v2 in Jupyter testing. The difference in accuracy is attributed to the model detecting entirely new images.

Android Smartphone Camera Testing.description: Testing performed using an Android-based smartphone camera (Realme 3, 3GB RAM, 13-megapixel camera) on 40 additional images not in the main dataset.
Android Smartphone Camera Testing.notes: MobileNet v2 and Faster R-CNN achieved the same highest accuracy of 86.3% on the smartphone, though at different optimal learning rates (0.0001 and 0.005 respectively). Accuracy on smartphone varied compared to Jupyter due to factors like light, camera capability, and distance.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were used for both MobileNet v2 and Faster R-CNN.
architecture_used.input_size: Original image size was maintained; no change in image size or resolution was applied during data collection.

MobileNet_v2.description: Designed for efficiency on embedded/mobile devices. Uses depthwise separable convolution as the primary layer to reduce feature numbers. Includes bottleneck layers and shortcut connections.

Faster_R_CNN.description: Deep learning model derived from Fast R-CNN with added Region Proposal Network (RPN). Offers faster computing than predecessors like YOLO and SSD.
components: Region Proposal Network (RPN) for object detection using sliding windows (n x n) to generate bounding boxes
ROI Pooling to reduce bounding boxes
Two fully connected layers for object classification (softmax activation) and coordinate prediction (linear activation)

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (learning rates were tuned, but optimizer type like SGD/Adam is not specified).
training_details.hardware: Windows 10, Nvidia GTX 1070 TI, 8 GB RAM, i5 Processor

classification_or_output_head.MobileNet_v2: Fully connected layers for classification (implied by accuracy reporting).
classification_or_output_head.Faster_R_CNN: Two fully connected layers: one for object class determination using softmax activation function, and one for object position coordinates using linear activation function.

preprocessing_techniques: Region of Interest (ROI) annotation using LabelImg to generate XML files containing bounding box coordinates for cancer targets
No resizing or resolution changes applied; original image size and resolution were maintained during data collection
No explicit color correction, denoising, filtering, or hair/artifact removal techniques reported
conclusions: The Faster R-CNN model achieved higher accuracy (87.2%) than MobileNet v2 (86.1%) when tested on original images via Jupyter notebook.
MobileNet v2 achieved the same high accuracy (86.3%) as Faster R-CNN (86.3%) when deployed and tested on an Android smartphone camera.
Both Faster R-CNN and MobileNet v2 are effective for mobile-based skin cancer screening, with MobileNet v2 demonstrating comparable performance to Faster R-CNN in a mobile deployment context.
Faster R-CNN demonstrated superior performance compared to MobileNet v2 during the training process and offline Jupyter notebook testing.
Accuracy results obtained via smartphone camera testing varied compared to Jupyter notebook testing due to factors such as lighting, camera capability, and distance between the camera and the object.
The models were able to overcome challenges related to the significant difference between the number of normal skin pixels and cancer skin pixels in the sample images.
Future work should focus on developing multi-class skin disease detection and blending with segmentation methods to improve accuracy.
important_remarks: The dataset is small (600 images) and lacks demographic diversity, as the authors explicitly state there was 'no regard for gender, age, or additional factors,' limiting generalizability.
The study only addresses two specific skin cancer types (actinic keratosis and melanoma), ignoring other common lesions like nevi or basal cell carcinoma, which limits clinical scope.

External validation is weak; smartphone testing was performed on only 40 additional images not in the main dataset, using a single device model (Realme 3), which may not represent broader hardware variability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; results are presented solely as accuracy percentages without measures of variance or statistical comparison.
The paper does not specify the optimizer used (e.g., SGD, Adam), only reporting learning rates, which hinders reproducibility of the training process.

Accuracy performance varied significantly between Jupyter notebook testing (offline) and smartphone camera testing (online) due to uncontrolled environmental factors like lighting, camera capability, and distance, highlighting deployment sensitivity.

The study relies on pre-trained models without detailing the source of pre-training weights or fine-tuning strategies beyond learning rate and step count, potentially introducing bias from the original training data.

The dataset was sourced from the ISIC archive, but the specific subset or version used is not detailed, raising concerns about data leakage if the test images overlap with standard ISIC benchmarks used in other studies.
